text:
# Hospitality: looking for a sustainable workforce

[Analysis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/analyses/index.html)

[Leisure](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/leisure/index.html)

27/05/2025 • 9 minutes reading

[Gerarda Westerhuis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/gerarda-westerhuis/index.html)

[Wieger Cosijn](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/wieger-cosijn/index.html)

The catering industry is a vital sector, but is structurally faced with staff shortages. The pressure on the sector is high, partly because it relies on young people with a part-time job. At the same time, the number of MBO and HBO students in catering courses is declining, which weakens the basis for sustainable personnel growth. Possible solutions include making better use of MBO students, using technology, and bringing in more lateral entrants.

The workforce in the catering industry has been under pressure for a long time. The sector has suffered from a high outflow of personnel due to the closures during the corona pandemic. The sector has since recovered well from this shock, and the percentages of created and filled vacancies have returned to their previous levels. It appears that the number of vacancies, namely 30,400 in the first quarter of 2025, is still high, but lower than the peak of 2022 and 2023.

The vacancy rate of 63, the number of vacancies per 1,000 jobs, also shows that the pressure is decreasing, but not over (see Figure 1). The economic survey by the Central Bureau of Statistics (CBS) from April 2025 shows that almost 40 percent of catering entrepreneurs still experience obstacles in the field of personnel. For example, almost 20 percent of restaurants and cafes limit their supply to the amount of labor available; for example, they are forced to close more often or earlier. Within the accommodation sector, which includes hotels and holiday parks, this concerns 12 percent of the companies.

metadata.source_title: Hospitality industry: looking for a sustainable workforce

text:
But catering entrepreneurs have not stood still. The aforementioned CBS survey also shows that more than a third of catering entrepreneurs aim for more attractive employment conditions, such as a higher salary, to attract staff. In addition, relatively many accommodation providers are focusing on automation to combat the staff shortage; 25 percent of accommodation providers compared to 10 percent of restaurant and café owners.

## Looking for a sustainable workforce

Young people are finding the catering industry as an employer again. Of the total workforce, approximately 58 percent consists of 15 to 25-year-olds, compared to 50 percent in 2015 (see Figure 2).

It is essential to distinguish between two types of young people who work in the catering industry. On the one hand, the part-time workers who temporarily strengthen the sector during their secondary school or studies and leave it after obtaining their diploma, and on the other hand, the MBO and HBO graduates who have opted for a study and career focused on the catering industry. While part-time workers are indispensable for flexibility and coping with peak workloads, the trained professionals ensure continuity and quality within the sector. It is important for catering entrepreneurs to retain this second group.

It is therefore a worrying development that the share of the group of 25 to 34 year olds has fallen from almost 19 percent in 2015 to 12 percent in 2024 (see Figure 2). The share of this group in the entire business community has been around 20 percent in the past ten years. In many cases, young people in the 15-25 year group apparently lack the perspective to continue working after their education and to make a career in the catering industry. The fact that the advancement of young people is stalling is also evident from the fact that the average age of departure in the catering industry is only 25.1 years.

Of the total workforce in the catering industry, a quarter has completed an MBO education at levels 2 to 4, compared to 34 percent of the total labor market. The share of HBO and university graduates within the catering industry is also considerably lower than the national average: 11 percent compared to 36 percent.

metadata.source_title: Hospitality industry: looking for a sustainable workforce

text:
At the same time, the number of catering and hospitality students at intermediate vocational and higher professional education level is decreasing significantly. From 2020 to 2024, this number fell by 15 percent, and government forecasts predict a further decline in these numbers. The [Hospitality Pact](https://www.hospitalitypact.nl/nl/) is a great initiative to make catering courses more attractive. It is a collaboration between companies, educational and knowledge institutions and the government and describes how the sector can achieve a healthy labor market. One of the spearheads is to ensure a sufficient influx of qualified and yet to be qualified talent into the labor market and into training courses.

Below we will first look in more detail at the two groups of young people and then at the use of technology and lateral entrants.

## Part-time jobs: flexible contracts and large outflow

The share of young people in the workforce is therefore large and goes hand in hand with a large share of flexible employment relationships in the catering industry (more than 63 percent). The sector has a large group of on-call workers (44 percent). Many employees need this flexibility, according to the 2024 National Working Conditions Survey (NEA); No fewer than 35 percent of employees in the catering industry indicate that they like flexibility. A third consider flexible employment relationships in the catering industry to be normal. Both percentages are significantly higher than the total for all sectors (20.8 and 20.7 respectively). The need for flexibility mainly applies to service (almost 41 percent), and slightly less among cooks and kitchen assistants (almost 32 percent).

The outflow in the catering sector has also traditionally been relatively high due to the large proportion of young people. The outflow amounts to approximately 35 percent of the total number of jobs in the sector per quarter (see Figure 4). Only the Rental and other business services sector has a higher share of outflows, at 40 percent, according to CBS data. The National Working Conditions Survey 2024 shows that catering employees have worked for their current employer for an average of 3.93 years; this number is the lowest of all sectors and substantially lower than the average of 8.73 for the entire business community.

## MBO students looking for clarity and stability

metadata.source_title: Hospitality industry: looking for a sustainable workforce

text:
## MBO students looking for clarity and stability

Practically trained MBO graduates are crucial for keeping the catering industry running, but prove difficult to retain. Recent research by ABN AMRO shows that the wishes of practically educated people from Generation Z (abbreviated 'Gen Z', born between 1997 and 2012), such as many MBO students in the catering industry, differ fundamentally from the stereotypical image of this generation as 'freedom-seeking idealists'. Entrepreneurs would therefore do well to adjust their image of this generation.

[Also read: What practically educated Gen Zers really want](/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/industrie/wat-praktisch-opleidinge-gen-zers-echt-wanten.html)

Young people in this group are mainly looking for clarity and stability. At the top of their priority list are a good salary and clear expectations about the future. This reveals an important challenge for catering entrepreneurs: many employment conditions in the catering industry are still uncertain, guidance is limited and the growth prospects are unclear. It is precisely these elements that are decisive for Gen Z.

At the same time, this generation also offers opportunities. Gen Zers are loyal if they are well guided and feel taken seriously. They want to do concrete work that they can be proud of, and the catering industry is ideally suited for that. From a well-organized lunch to working a busy evening shift: young people appreciate the direct results of their efforts.

Increasing the appeal to Gen Zers and then retaining them requires more clarity in the offer. Young people value transparent information about salary, responsibilities and development opportunities. A good start on the work floor, with attention to guidance, contributes to their motivation and loyalty. In addition, it can help when young employees are involved in parts of the team or business operations, so that they experience ownership and appreciation.

## Investing in technology; change in skills

metadata.source_title: Hospitality industry: looking for a sustainable workforce

text:
## Investing in technology; change in skills

The structurally tight labor market and increasing workload stimulate increasing productivity. This can be done by investing in technology or by providing training to staff. Accommodation providers appear to be further along in this regard than restaurants: 23 percent of accommodation providers invest in technology and automation, compared to 11 percent of restaurants. This is evident from the CBS Business Survey of April 2025. Optimization of the working environment and facilities is applied by 29 percent of accommodation providers, while 25 percent of restaurants opt for this. Around a quarter of both accommodation providers and restaurants are implementing more efficient work processes. Training and development programs are provided by 32 percent of accommodation providers, compared to 24 percent of restaurants.

Hotels, which fall within the accommodation providers, are generally more advanced in digitalization than restaurants, for several reasons. First, the processes in hotels are more complex, requiring integrated systems for managing reservations, check-in and check-out, billing and guest communications. Restaurants, on the other hand, have simpler processes such as reservations, serving and payment, which means that digitalization is often fragmented and less integrated. In addition, hotels have more contact moments with customers; both before, during and after the stay. These interactions are more intensive and long-lasting than the shorter customer relationships in restaurants, making digital tools for experience and upselling in hotels particularly valuable.

For both hotels and restaurants, larger chains are often further advanced in the degree of digitalization and automation than smaller companies. They usually have more available resources to invest in advanced technologies. They can also roll out the systems across different locations, which creates economies of scale. Moreover, the need for larger chains is greater due to the more complex processes, while smaller companies can sometimes continue to run their simple business operations more efficiently without heavy digitalization.

metadata.source_title: Hospitality industry: looking for a sustainable workforce

text:
Technological innovation not only transforms the working methods of catering companies, but also places new demands on the staff who have to use this technology on a daily basis. A report by [Pantour](https://nexttourismgeneration.eu/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/PANTOUR-COUNTRY-SKILLS-PROFILE-REPORTS_NETHERLANDS_ENGLISH.pdf) (Pact for Next Tourism Generation Skills) shows that there is a significant gap between the current level of skills and the expected level required in 2030. While basic skills such as Office applications and digital (email) marketing is well developed, the biggest challenges lie with the more advanced digital applications, especially in the areas of generative AI and data analysis.

Especially in a sector that revolves around experience, it remains important to maintain the balance between efficiency and personal contact. A friendly welcome, dealing with unexpected situations and creating a warm atmosphere remain distinctive skills.

## A third of catering employers are open to lateral entrants

Recent research by [UWV](https://www.werk.nl/imagesdxa/kwart_werkgevers_nam_zij-instromers_aan_27022024_tcm95-455728.pdf) shows that 24 percent of catering employers hired lateral entrants in the past two years; slightly below the average for all sectors of 27 percent. In addition, 30 percent of catering employers are open to lateral entry, compared to 32 percent on average. A lateral entry is someone with work experience in another professional group who is hired for a position that has educational requirements that still require training. What is striking is that more than half of the catering employers (52 percent) indicate that lateral entry does not apply, because there are no positions for which training requirements apply; this percentage is much higher than the average of 29 percent.

The catering industry therefore offers many accessible entry options. Functions such as waiter or kitchen assistant often do not require formal diplomas, which makes the transition from other sectors easier. Hospitality employers who do have positions with training requirements are more than average open to lateral entry. These may be positions that require only limited training, such as a social hygiene certificate, or positions that require new digital skills or management skills, for example.

metadata.source_title: Hospitality industry: looking for a sustainable workforce

text:
At the same time, there are clear bottlenecks. Smaller catering establishments in particular often lack the capacity to properly guide lateral entrants. Employers also fear that investments in training will be lost if employees leave quickly. That is a real risk in a sector with high employee turnover. In addition, unlike healthcare or construction, for example, the catering industry has few formally structured lateral entry pathways.

Such programs are learn-work programs set up by, for example, trade organizations, educational institutions or government. They consist of an intake, training, guidance and job guarantee. These processes are more common in healthcare, education and construction than in the catering industry, where inflow often takes place more informally and ad hoc. An interesting example that is not only intended for current employees who want to grow, but also for new talent, is the HBO course in Hotel Management (associate degree) from NHL Stenden University of Applied Sciences. The course offers a combination of working and learning, and admission is possible via a 21-plus assessment. This provides an accessible route to a managerial position in the catering industry.

The catering industry offers many opportunities for lateral entrants, precisely because of the low entry barrier and the willingness to give people with a different background a chance. At the same time, a more sustainable use of lateral entry requires more structure: short-term training, guidance on the work floor and possibly sector-wide retraining programs.

## Conclusion

The staff shortages in the catering industry require more than short-term solutions. By investing more consciously in MBO graduates, using technology smartly and better guiding lateral entrants, the sector can build a more stable and sustainable workforce.

## Read more in the Leisure sector

The Leisure sector has largely gone through a difficult period. The recovery has started with the phasing out of the measures regarding corona. The terraces are starting to fill up, the number of tourists is increasing and events can be organized again. Staff shortages are now the biggest challenge. Digitizing staff work and spreading guests can limit the impact.
